coredata.dc:title: Lipid-coated red fluorescent carbon dots for imaging and synergistic phototherapy in breast cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Shinde, Vinod Ravasaheb
Khatun, Sajmina
Thanekar, Ajinkya Madhukar
Hak, Arshadul
Rengan, Aravind Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of carbon dots using plant leaves is a facile and economically viable approach. Here we report the development of lipid-coated red fluorescent carbon dots (LRCDs), a biocompatible and stable nanomaterial, utilizing Clitoria ternatea leaves. The red fluorescent carbon dots (RCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method, followed by lipid coating using rotary evaporation for imaging-guided phototherapy. RCDs generate heat in tandem with NIR laser irradiation and could therefore be employed as a photothermal agent in cancer therapy. Additionally, the fluorescent nature of RCDs can be utilized in bioimaging. The fabricated RCDs displayed a characteristic fluorescent emission maximum at 672 nm with a shoulder peak at 723 nm. Hydrophobicity is a major drawback associated with the RCDs, which limits their therapeutic efficiency due to poor biodistribution and rapid clearance. To address this limitation, we coated RCDs with soya lecithin to generate hydrophilic LRCDs with better bioavailability and therapeutic effectiveness. Further analysis using MTT assay reveals high biocompatibility and a distinct photothermal ablation potency of LRCDs against L929 and 4T1 cells, respectively. LRCDs could potentially be synthesized on a large scale and used for a variety of applications due to their low-cost, and biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red fluorescent carbon dots
Hydrothermal method
Phototherapy
Bioimaging
Clitoria ternatea

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of LRCDs by rotary evaporation method
PTT efficiency of NPs with 4T1 cells
In ovo PTT efficacy of LRCDs (CAM assay)
Biocompatibility and bioimaging studies with zebrafish
In ovo PTT efficacy of LRCDs (CAM assay)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs. (a) UV absorbance spectra of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs. (b) LRCDs fluorescence spectra. LRCDs were shown remarkable excitation peak at 408 nm and emission peaks at 672 nm with a shoulder peak at 723 nm. (c) LP, RCDs, and LRCDs photographs under UV-visible light (365 nm). (d) Hydrodynamic diameter analysis of LRCDs by size analyser. TEM images of synthesized RCDs, and LRCDs (e), and (f), respectively.
(a) XPS survey spectra of RCDs. (b) C1s XPS spectrum for carbon species of RCDs. (c) O1s XPS spectrum for oxygen species of RCDs. (d) FTIR analysis LP, RCDs, and LRCDs, respectively.
(a) Photothermal transduction of Control (PBS), LP, RCDs, and LRCDs. (b) Photothermal transduction of Control, LP, RCDs, and LRCDs.

Biocompatibility of (a) LP, (b) RCDs, and (c) LRCDs on L929 after 24 h of incubation. (d) Intracellular uptake of Control, LP, RCDs, and LRCDs with 4T1 cells. Images captured by Olympus microscope; Scale bar 100 µm. (e) Intracellular uptake of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs quantitatively evaluated upon incubation for 6 h.

(a) Live/Dead assay with 4T1 cells followed by laser irradiation. Images were taken by Olympus fluorescence microscope. (b) Quantitative analysis of Live and Dead cells (b) and (c), respectively; Scale bar 100 µm.

(a) Control, LP, RCDs, and LRCDs stained by DCFDA upon laser irradiation for five mins. Images captured by Olympus microscope (Scale bar 100 µm). (b) Quantitative estimation of ROS generated by LP, RCDs, and LRCDs using DCFDA assay. (c) Laser-mediated cell cytotoxicity in the presence and absence of NIR irradiation continued with MTT assay after 24 h of incubation.

In ovo studies. (a) Control, LP, RCDs, and LRCDs uptake in zebrafish embryos at various time lengths (0, 6, 12, 24, and 72 h). (Scale bar 100 µm). (b) Quantitative analysis of Control, LP, RCDs, and LRCDs uptake in zebrafish.
In ovo CAM assay, CAM treated with LP, RCDs, and LRCDs with NIR laser-mediated blood vessels damage.

head.ce:title: Lipid-coated red fluorescent carbon dots for imaging and synergistic phototherapy in breast cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad, Kandi, Telangana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad, Kandi, Telangana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of carbon dots using plant leaves is a facile and economically viable approach. Here we report the development of lipid-coated red fluorescent carbon dots (LRCDs), a biocompatible and stable nanomaterial, utilizing Clitoria ternatea leaves. The red fluorescent carbon dots (RCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method, followed by lipid coating using rotary evaporation for imaging-guided phototherapy. RCDs generate heat in tandem with NIR laser irradiation and could therefore be employed as a photothermal agent in cancer therapy. Additionally, the fluorescent nature of RCDs can be utilized in bioimaging. The fabricated RCDs displayed a characteristic fluorescent emission maximum at 672 nm with a shoulder peak at 723 nm. Hydrophobicity is a major drawback associated with the RCDs, which limits their therapeutic efficiency due to poor biodistribution and rapid clearance. To address this limitation, we coated RCDs with soya lecithin to generate hydrophilic LRCDs with better bioavailability and therapeutic effectiveness. Further analysis using MTT assay reveals high biocompatibility and a distinct photothermal ablation potency of LRCDs against L929 and 4T1 cells, respectively. LRCDs could potentially be synthesized on a large scale and used for a variety of applications due to their low-cost, and biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is the most common cancer type in females. Based on cellular markers, breast cancer is broadly categorized into three types: (i) ER/PR positive, (ii) HER2, and (iii) triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). Among these subtypes, the most aggressive form of breast cancer with high recurrence and the lowest survival rate is TNBC [1,2]. The current treatment options for TNBC typically relies upon surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, but these therapies are associated with major limitations like severe side effect, high resistance, and relapse [3]. In recent years, phototherapy has drawn huge attention in cancer treatment as an alternative modality of conventional therapies due to its minimal invasiveness, high selectivity, and lower toxicity [4,5]. Phototherapy involves photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT), which require an external light source and light-activating agents, known as photosensitizers and photothermal agents [6]. In PTT, photothermal agents are employed to destroy cancer cells by photothermal ablation, while in PDT, light-activated photosensitizers generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) [7,8]. Metallic nanoparticles such as Au, Ag, Cu, Fe, Pt, and Ti nanoparticles, have been extensively investigated in PTT owing to their superior photothermal effects [9–15]. Dai et al. reported the synthesis of a novel organic dye BDF1005, using boron difluoride formazanate (BDF) for NIR-II fluorescence imaging-guided PTT. The dye was encapsulated in PS-g-PEG nanoparticles, and which exhibited excellent image-guided PTT activity in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice [16]. However, metallic nanoparticles generate high toxicity that induces side effects like oxidative stress and inflammatory responses [17,18].

Lipid coating over nanoparticle surface is an excellent approach to improve biocompatibility, dispersity, and uptake by the cancer cells. Khakbaz et al. reported the combination of doxorubicin and CDs for combined chemo-photothermal therapy. Here, doxorubicin and CDs were loaded together in the niosomes and the combined effects of chemotherapy and PTT showed better cytotoxicity in breast cancer cells [19]. Acona et al. reported the synthesis of zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles, coated with a double-lipidic layer, for PDT. The lipid on ZnO nanoparticles improved their colloidal stability and internalization by HeLa cells which resulted in promising PDT outcomes [20]. Kang et al. reported lipid-coated gold nanoparticles for PTT. The nanoparticles were coated with thermosensitive phospholipids, and docetaxel was further loaded into the lipid bilayer, which improved the anticancer activity of this nanosystem due to their synergistic effects [21]. Similar to this, thermoresponsive phospholipid-coated superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles have also been reported by Allam et al. for PTT-based combination therapy. Here camptothecin was encapsulated into the phospholipid bilayer to induce synergistic anticancer effects against human Jurkat lymphoma cells [22]. In addition to metallic nanoparticles, lipid coating has also been reported for nanoscale metal-organic frameworks (nanoMOFs). In a study reported by Li et al., gemcitabine-monophosphate loaded iron trimesate nanoMOFs were coated with the combination of DSPE-PEG 2000 and DOPC. The lipid coating over nanoMOFs provided better control over drug release and helped to reduce macrophage uptake, which resulted in effective cancer cell killing [23].

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of photothermal agents, known for their high biocompatibility, thermostability, photostability, and photothermal conversion efficacy. CDs are also fluorescent in nature, which makes them a potential candidate for imaging-guided PTT [24,25]. Geng et al. reported the synthesis of nitrogen and oxygen co-doped CDs (NO-CDs) for bioimaging and PTT. These NO-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal molecular fusion process with 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and branched polyethyleneimine. With desired biocompatibility and photostability, the NO-CDs showed excellent PTT effects both in vitro and in vivo against HeLa cells [26]. In a similar study, Yuting et al. reported a one-pot synthesis of CDs by the pyrolysis of glycerine and polydopamine. Polydopamine is useful for the doping of N atoms, which enhances the fluorescence of CDs. Furthermore, the presence of polydopamine improves PTT outcomes due to its high photothermal conversion efficacy [27]. In addition to PDT, CDs were employed in PDT due to their ROS production ability. Wang et al. developed Cu-doped CDs with good biocompatibility and high singlet oxygen (1O2) quantum yield. Under laser irradiation, the Cu-CDs emitted green fluorescence and generated 1O2 -mediated cytotoxicity, which induced effective cell death in HeLa and SH-SY5Y cells [28]. In cancer therapeutics, combination of two or more therapies is always more effective than any monotherapy due to their synergistic effects. Recent studies have reported the development of novel CDs which exhibit combined PDT and PTT effects. Bai et al. synthesized S and N co-doped CDs, which showed exceptional therapeutic efficiency due to their synergistic phototherapy outcomes [29]. Another study reported Hypocrella bambusae derived CDs for imaging-guided synergistic PDT/PTT against HeLa cells. This study provides a novel strategy for the synthesis of CDs using natural carbon sources, which can overcome the limitations associated with synthetic precursors [30].

In this study, we report the synthesis of red fluorescent carbon dots (RCDs) using hydrothermal method for imaging-based synergistic PTT and PDT. The RCDs were prepared by Clitoria ternatea leaves, which is a facile and cost-effective approach to preparing carbon-based nanomaterials. Hydrophobicity is a major drawback associated with RCDs that limits their therapeutic potential. Therefore, we coated the RCDs with soya lecithin using rotary evaporation method to prepare lipid-coated red fluorescent carbon dots (LRCDs). The LRCDs showed improved solubility and better biocompatibility in comparison to the RCDs. Moreover, lipid coating enhanced the cellular uptake of LRCDs, which generated higher cytotoxicity in 4T1 cells. The biocompatibility and fluorescence imaging of LRCDs were also investigated in ovo using zebrafish embryos. The LRCDs did not affect the embryonic development and showed time-dependent enhancement of fluorescence intensity. This work provides a novel platform for imaging-guided synergistic PTT and PDT for the treatment of breast cancer.

Clitoria ternatea leaves, Soya lecithin, ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol, dichloromethane (DCM), and chloroform were purchased from SRL Chemicals, India. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), DMEM media, RPMI media, [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] (MTT) were purchased from HiMedia Chemicals, Mumbai, India. Polyether sulfone (PES) membrane filters Sartorius of 0.2 µm cut-off size were purchased from Carrigtwohill, Ireland. Millipore water was acquired from Millipore, Billerica, MA.

The RCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Fresh Clitoria ternatea leaves paste was prepared by mortar pestle, followed by dispersion in ethanol, and stirred for 4 h (h) on a magnetic stirrer. Thereafter, the solution was filtered with filter paper. The filtered solution was brought into a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave (100 mL) and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 24 h. After cooling down, the product was filtrated through a 0.2 µm polyether sulfone membrane to obtain a dark-green solution. This solution was desiccated by rotary evaporation. The crude product was added into a mixed solution of DCM and distilled water with a ratio of 1:1 for extraction. After standing and layering, the dark green solutions obtained which contain RCDs [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of LRCDs by rotary evaporation method
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 20 mg soya lecithin dissolved in 3 mL chloroform and 1 mg RCDs were mixed with 2 mL methanol. The organic solvent was (chloroform and methanol) evaporated by rotary evaporation method. Followed by, thin film hydration which was carried out by adding distilled water, and sample was stored at 4 °C for further analysis [32].

The fluorescence of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs was assessed using a UV lamp (365 nm). The absorption spectra were measured on UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800, Japan). The excitation and emission spectra for LRCDs were recorded on a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu RF 6000, Japan). The hydrodynamic size of the LRCDs was measured by particle size analyser (Inc. Santa Barbara, Calf. USA). The size and morphology of RCDs and LRCDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (JEOL F200, USA). Chemical bond configurations and elemental composition of RCDs detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (KRATOS Analytical, UK). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify the functional groups (Shimadzu, FT/IR-4600typeA, Japan).

Photothermal transduction of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs were assessed. 30 µg/mL of the as-prepared LP, RCDs, and LRCDs solution were exposed to laser (690 nm, 650 mW) irradiation at different time gaps (0, 3, 5,7, and 10 min). PBS was used as a control. A thermal camera was used to take the corresponding thermal images as well as the initial and final temperature (before and after irradiation). Total 5 cycles of laser irradiation were repeated on same samples for detection of photothermal stability. By using a thermal imaging camera thermal images and temperature were captured. After 10 min laser irradiation of all samples for 5 cycles images were obtained by the end of 5th cycle.

Murine triple-negative breast cancer (4T1) and fibroblast (L929) cells were nourished in DMEM and RPMI medium added with 100 U/mL streptomycin/penicillin and 10% (v/v) FBS in a moistened condition at 37 °C with 5% CO2, respectively.

The MTT assay was utilized to investigate the biocompatibility of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs on normal fibroblast cell lines L929. 7 × 105 cells were seeded on a cell culture plate and cultured for 24 h. The following day, cells were treated with varied doses of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs. The growth media was plain DMEM containing MTT (5 mg/mL), and cells were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. After dissolving the formazan crystals in DMSO, the absorbance at 570 nm was measured with a microplate reader (PerkinElmer, USA).

4T1 cells were seeded on top of coverslip and cultured in 12 well plates. After 24 h incubations, cells were treated with 20 μg/ml of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs and incubated for 6 h. Subsequently, cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min following PBS wash. ZOE™ fluorescent cell imager (BIO-RAD) was used for capturing images.

Live/Dead assay was used to compare PTT/PDT-mediated LRCDs cell cytotoxicity to RCDs and LP. A 96-well plate was used to grow 4T1 cells. The cells and the control group were treated with nanoparticles with the same concentration after the 24 h incubation period. Cell cytotoxicity with (5 mins/well) and without NIR irradiation was evaluated after 2 h of incubation. Fluorescein diacetate (FDA) and propidium iodide (PI) were used to stain the live and dead cell populations and fluorescence microscopy (Olympus CKX-53 USA) was used to take images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PTT efficiency of NPs with 4T1 cells
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded at a density of 5 × 103 cells/well in a microplate to evaluate photothermal efficiency. Following day cells were treated by LP, RCDs, and LRCDs with the same concentration for 4 h. Subsequently 7 h incubation cell viability was then evaluated using an MTT assay after the cells have been subjected to a 690 nm laser irradiation for 5 min.

4T1 cells were seeded at 5 × 103 cells/well in 96-well plates and maintained in RPMI. Fresh medium containing LP, RCDs, and LRCDs was added 24 h later. After treatment, 10 µM 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFDA) was added, and cells were kept for incubation. ROS generations were evaluated before and after NIR laser irradiation. Fluorescence measurements were taken with the help of microplate reader (Excitation: 485 nm and Emission: 535). Images were captured by fluorescence microscope.

The singlet oxygen formation was studied. The 5 × 104 4T1 cells/well were seeded in microplate. After 24 h incubation, the old media was replaced by fresh media containing LP, RCDs, and LRCDs and kept for 4 h incubation. After nanoparticle treatment, Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green (SOS G) dye was added to cells. Then cells were irradiated with NIR laser for 5 min, images were captured under the fluorescence microscope before and after laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In ovo PTT efficacy of LRCDs (CAM assay)
ce:section.ce:para: The in ovo CAM assay was conducted using fertilized chicken eggs which were obtained from the local Kandi village, Hyderabad. The eggs were incubated horizontally under shaking conditions (HHD-24S Digital Egg incubator, Made in India) at 37 ºC with 70% humidity for about 4 days. Post the incubation period, the formation of blood vessels was visualized using a hand camera. Under sterile conditions, the eggshell was opened on the snub side with the help of forceps and a needle, and about 2-3 mL of albumin content was removed from the egg using a syringe. The hole was further sealed with a thin parafilm. On the opposite end, where the blood vessels were visible, a small incision was made with the help of needles and forceps. The NIR light-mediated PTT effect of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs was evaluated on these eggs. About 100 µg/mL of each particle was injected into the blood vessels, incubated for 2 h, and then exposed to a 690 nm laser for 10 min. Post the treatment, the blood vessels were observed and captured using the hand camera to detect their PTT effect.

A red and light purple fluorescence of RCDs and LRCDs were observed under the UV lamp (365 nm), respectively. The optical properties of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs were evaluated using UV-visible absorption spectra. RCDs and LRCDs exhibited absorption peaks at the same wavelength at 408 nm as shown in Fig. 1 . This might be due to π-π* (C=C) and n-π* (C=O) transition groups [33]. When RCDs and LRCDs were used for fluorescence spectroscopy detection, remarkable emission peaks were observed at 672 nm with a shoulder peak 723 nm (excitation 408) in both RCDs and LRCDs confirming the stability of nanoparticles [34,35]. The plain liposome average hydrodynamic size around 130 nm as shown in Fig. S1. LRCDs having a narrow distribution size and an average hydrodynamic size 210 nm. The TEM analysis revealed the spherical morphology of RCDs and a particle size distribution ranging from 5 to 10 nm.

The surface composition and chemical bond configurations of RCDs were determined using XPS analysis. The elemental composition of RCDs is C (87.30), O (12.20), and N (0.6%) detected as per XPS survey data. However, carbon content of RCDs is significantly higher, indicating that RCDs have a higher degree of carbonization. The C1s and O1s XPS curves were refined for analysis. RCDs C1s spectra bear CC/C=C (283.1 eV), C=N (284.1 eV), CO (284.9 eV), and C=N (286.6 eV) however, the O1s spectra contain C=O (529.6 eV) then CO (533.4 eV). Based on the XPS curves (Fig. 2 a–c) of C1s and O1s, it could be concluded that RCDs can be a higher conjugated system with a higher graphitization degree [31,36,37].

The infrared band at 3400–2850 cm−1, showed the long alkyl chain (CH stretching) and aromatic rings, also RCDs significant showed carbonyl (C=O) stretching structure at approximately 1733–1653 cm−1, C=C stretching vibration at 1457 cm−1 (Fig. 2d). The visible light absorption and emission in the NIR range are the proof of RCDs having bulky conjugated systems along with aromatic rings [31,38–40]. In the case of LRCDs, the IR band 3446–2924 cm−1 shifting indicated the coating of LP with RCDs.

LP, RCDs, and LRCDs were exposed with NIR laser (690 nm) irradiation; LRCDs demonstrated more potent photothermal transduction than LP and RCDs. Laser irradiated LRCDs showed temperature elevation above 47 °C at 5 min, indicating enhancement in photothermal effect. The elevated temperature for 10 mins was maintained using stable NIR laser and the temperature decline was observed at 10 mins. When the RCDs were treated for 5 mins with a NIR laser, the temperature raised upto 41 °C followed by slow decline to 37 °C after continuous irradiation (Fig. 3 a,b). This temperature decline might be caused due to photo-degradation of RCDs. Moreover, the thermal stability of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs were investigated using NIR laser irradiation on/off cycles. The temperature rise of LP was consistent across all five cycles. The temperature of RCDs dropped from 46 ± 1.4 °C in the first cycle to 43 ± 0.8 °C in the third cycle, ensuring the biodegradable property RCDs after a certain time point of thermal irradiation (Fig. S3). The temperature of LRCDs remained stable until the third cycle when it decreased slightly from 46 ± 1 to 45 ± 0.3 °C. These findings suggested that LRCDs have a stable photothermal transduction effect [41].

First, the in vitro biocompatibility of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs, were determined using standard MTT colorimetric assays at various concentrations (from 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 µg/mL). According to Fig. 4 a and c after 24 h of incubation, more than 90% of L929 cells were viable, demonstrating the high biocompatibility of LP and LRCDs [42].

The ZOE™ fluorescent cell imager was used to investigate the in vitro fluorescent imaging capabilities of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs. 4T1 cells were treated by LP, RCDs, and LRCDs (20 µg/mL−1) and kept for 6 h incubation. 4T1 cells emit red fluorescence confirming the cellular uptake of LRCDs as shown in Fig. 4d and e [43,44].

The 4T1 cells were subjected to LP, RCDs LRCDs subsequent 5 min-NIR light irradiation. Significant cell ablation (60 ± 0.8%) was observed, confirming the therapeutic efficiency. After treatment 4T1 cells were double-stained by FDA and PI fluorescent dyes. In the LRCDs + NIR light group, more cell death was observed (Fig. 5 ), whereas Control, LP + NIR light groups showed live cells. Therefore, LRCDs could be a promising photothermal agent for therapeutic applications [45].

The presence of green fluorescence indicated ROS generation in 4T1 cells. After 5 min of NIR laser therapy, RCDs and LRCDs showed enhanced intensity of green fluorescence. This confirms the ability of LRCDs to generate ROS in cells and the subsequent PDT effect. As represented in Fig. 6 a,b, the DCFDA quantitative analysis results suggested that the ROS production increased after being treated with LRCDs compared to the control. LRCDs when irradiated with NIR light significantly generate ROS which confirmed the PDT effect [46].

4T1 Cells were treated with LP, RCDs, and LRCDs followed by NIR laser irradiated for 5 mins. After treatment cell survival rate in the LRCDs group decreased. Approximately 50% of cell death takes place after laser irradiation, suggesting that LRCDs were able to create sufficient heat to kill cancer cells with a good PTT effect as shown in Fig. 6c [47].

Singlet oxygen (1O2) production efficiency of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs was evaluated in real-time using SOS G dye after NIR laser irradiation. The 4T1 cells were irradiated with laser after treatment with LP, RCDs, and LRCDs for 5 min. In LRCDs treated groups a significant green fluorescence appeared shown in Fig. S3 owing to the SOS G activation. Green fluorescence in LRCDs treated group confirmed this structure having the ability to generate 1O2 in the presence of NIR irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and bioimaging studies with zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Zebrafish (Danio rerio) embryos were used to test the toxicity and bioimaging of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs. After post-fertilization, the embryos were collected for the experiment. The presence of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs did not interfere with the physical development of embryos and normal growth was observed (Fig. S4), indicating the biocompatibility of LRCDs in ovo zebrafish embryos. The distribution of LP, RCDs, and LRCDs in zebrafish was visualized through a fluorescence microscope [48,49]. Embryos were incubated with LP, RCDs, and LRCDs for different time points. Bright red fluorescence was observed at 6 h and enhanced with increase time points, depicting that LRCDs can efficiently accumulate in zebrafish embryos as shown in Fig. 7 a,b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In ovo PTT efficacy of LRCDs (CAM assay)
ce:section.ce:para: The PTT effect of LRCDs was evaluated using CAM assay. It was observed that the chick embryos treated with LRCDs, under laser irradiation, displayed evident rupturing of blood vessels compared with their controls, as shown in Fig. 8 . This might also be due to the heat being generated upon PTT. Thus, LRCDs function as a prospective photothermal agent capable of disrupting neoblood vessels [41,50,51].

In summary, red fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from Clitoria ternatea leaves by hydrothermal method. The hydrophobic RCDs were coated with soya lecithin to prepare hydrophilic LRCDs for synergistic photodynamic-photothermal therapy. The LRCDs have shown high fluorescence stability and biocompatibility. However, the fluorescent nature of RCDs was utilized in bioimaging. Treatment of 4T1 cells with LRCDs under laser irradiation exhibited excellent cell killing through photothermal ablation and ROS-mediated cytotoxicity. The CAM assay results revealed that LRCDs with NIR radiation exhibited strong PTT effect. Due to their exceptional optical characteristics and high biocompatibility, we observed that LRCDs are viable choice for use as an effective phototherapy agent. Our study suggests that LRCDs could be a promising phototheranostic agent for effective cancer treatment with fluorescence imaging.

Vinod Ravasaheb Shinde: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sajmina Khatun: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Ajinkya Madhukar Thanekar: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology. Arshadul Hak: Writing – original draft. Aravind Kumar Rengan: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the following research funding agency: DST/TDT/SHRI-34/2021, DBT NNT (BT/NNT/28/1386/2017), ICMR (grant no. 35/1/2020-GIA/Nano/BMS), and SERB-CRG (CRG/2020/005069) for research funding. Author AMT and VRS would like to acknowledge the Council of Scientific Industrial Research (CSIR) (09/1001(0080)/2020-EMR-I) and (09/1001 (0064)/2019-EMR-I) for the financial support, respectively. Author SK would like to thank University Grants Commission (UGC) (18–340769) for UGC-JRF fellowship. The authors would also like to thank Ananya Padmakumar for her valuable suggestions. Graphical abstract was created using BioRender.com.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of triple-negative breast cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Kumar, R. Aggarwal, An overview of triple-negative breast cancer, Arch. Gynecol. Obstet. 293 (2016) 247–269. 10.1007/s00404-015-3859-y.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triple negative breast cancer and non-small cell lung cancer: clinical challenges and nano-formulation approaches

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ghosh, A. Javia, S. Shetty, D. Bardoliwala, K. Maiti, S. Banerjee, A. Khopade, A. Misra, K. Sawant, S. Bhowmick, Triple negative breast cancer and non-small cell lung cancer: clinical challenges and nano-formulation approaches, J. Control. Release 337 (2021) 27–58. 10.1016/j.jconrel.2021.07.014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metastatic and triple-negative breast cancer: challenges and treatment options

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.A. Mahmood, J. Sapiezynski, O.B. Garbuzenko, T. Minko, Metastatic and triple-negative breast cancer: challenges and treatment options, Drug Deliv. Transl. Res. 8 (2018) 1483–1507. 10.1007/s13346-018-0551-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasmonic MoO3-x nanoparticles incorporated in Prussian blue frameworks exhibit highly efficient dual photothermal/photodynamic therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.H. Odda, Y. Xu, J. Lin, G. Wang, N. Ullah, A. Zeb, K. Liang, L.P. Wen, A.W. Xu, Plasmonic MoO3-x nanoparticles incorporated in Prussian blue frameworks exhibit highly efficient dual photothermal/photodynamic therapy, J. Mater. Chem. B 7 (2019) 2032–2042. 10.1039/c8tb03148g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photodynamic therapy of cancer: an update

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Agostinis, K. Berg, K.A. Cengel, T.H. Foster, A.W. Girotti, S.O. Gollnick, S.M. Hahn, M.R. Hamblin, A. Juzeniene, D. Kessel, M. Korbelik, J. Moan, P. Mroz, D.
